summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

In a survey of 200 students, 80 prefer basketball. What is the relative frequency of students who prefer basketball?

Relative frequency = frequency / total = 80/200 = 0.40 = 40%.

A joint relative frequency in a two-way table is 0.15. If the total number of observations is 400, what is the joint frequency?

Joint frequency = joint relative frequency x total = 0.15 x 400 = 60.

A class of 50 students is surveyed. 15 are male and ride the bus. What is the joint relative frequency of male bus riders?

Joint relative frequency = joint frequency / grand total = 15/50 = 0.30 or 30%.

In a two-way table, 60 out of 100 males prefer action movies. What is the conditional relative frequency of preferring action movies given that the person is male?

Conditional relative frequency = joint frequency / marginal frequency of the condition = 60/100 = 0.60 or 60% of males prefer action movies.

A two-way table has a joint relative frequency of 0.12 for 'Male and Plays Soccer.' The marginal relative frequency for 'Male' is 0.48. What is the conditional relative frequency of playing soccer given male?

Conditional relative frequency = joint relative frequency / marginal relative frequency = 0.12 / 0.48 = 0.25 or 25% of males play soccer.

What is relative frequency?

Relative frequency is the ratio of a particular frequency to the total number of observations. It expresses what proportion or percentage of the total a particular category represents.

What is a joint relative frequency?

A joint relative frequency is found by dividing the frequency in a specific cell (the intersection of two categories) by the grand total of all observations in the table.

What is a marginal relative frequency?

A marginal relative frequency is the ratio of a row total or column total to the grand total, representing the proportion of observations that fall into that single category regardless of the other variable.

What is a conditional relative frequency?

A conditional relative frequency is found by dividing a joint frequency by a marginal frequency (row or column total), showing the proportion of one category within a specific subgroup of the other variable.

What is the difference between a frequency table and a relative frequency table?

A frequency table displays raw counts of observations, while a relative frequency table converts those counts to proportions or percentages by dividing each frequency by the total number of observations.

What is the difference between row conditional relative frequencies and column conditional relative frequencies?

Row conditional relative frequencies divide each cell by its row total (conditioning on the row variable), while column conditional relative frequencies divide each cell by its column total (conditioning on the column variable).

A restaurant surveys 500 customers about dining preference (Dine-In, Takeout) and day of week (Weekday, Weekend). Weekday Dine-In=120, Weekday Takeout=180, Weekend Dine-In=130, Weekend Takeout=70. What is the joint relative frequency of Weekend Dine-In customers?

Joint relative frequency = 130/500 = 0.26 or 26% of all customers are weekend dine-in customers.

A high school tracks 800 students by grade (Underclassman, Upperclassman) and extracurricular participation (Active, Inactive). Underclassman Active=180, Underclassman Inactive=220, Upperclassman Active=280, Upperclassman Inactive=120. What is the conditional relative frequency of being active given that the student is an upperclassman?

Upperclassman total: 280 + 120 = 400. Conditional relative frequency of active given upperclassman = 280/400 = 0.70 or 70%.

A fitness center surveys 300 members about class preference (Yoga, Spin, Weights) and time slot (Morning, Evening). Morning: Yoga=50, Spin=30, Weights=40. Evening: Yoga=40, Spin=60, Weights=80. What is the marginal relative frequency of evening members?

Evening total: 40 + 60 + 80 = 180. Marginal relative frequency = 180/300 = 0.60 or 60% of members prefer evening classes.

A hospital surveys 500 patients about visit type (Emergency, Scheduled) and insurance status (Insured, Uninsured). Emergency Insured=80, Emergency Uninsured=120, Scheduled Insured=250, Scheduled Uninsured=50. What is the conditional relative frequency of being uninsured given that the visit was an emergency?

Emergency total: 80 + 120 = 200. Conditional relative frequency of uninsured given emergency = 120/200 = 0.60 or 60%.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A car dealership surveys 600 buyers about vehicle type (SUV, Sedan, Truck) and payment method (Finance, Cash). Finance: SUV=150, Sedan=100, Truck=80. Cash: SUV=70, Sedan=120, Truck=80. The dealership manager says 'Truck buyers are equally likely to pay cash or finance.' Is this correct?

Truck buyers who finance: 80. Truck buyers who pay cash: 80. The manager is correct that truck buyers are equally split between financing and cash (50%/50%).

summary_sections.title: Special Topics
summary_sections.content_html:
Important Concepts

In a survey of 200 students, 80 prefer basketball. What is the relative frequency of students who prefer basketball

Relative frequency = frequency / total = 80/200 = 0.40 = 40%.

A joint relative frequency in a two-way table is 0.15. If the total number of observations is 400, what is the joint frequency

Joint frequency = joint relative frequency x total = 0.15 x 400 = 60.

In a two-way relative frequency table, the sum of all joint relative frequencies equals:

In a relative frequency table where each cell is divided by the grand total, all the joint relative frequencies sum to 1 (or 100%), because they represent all possible combinations.

A class of 50 students is surveyed. 15 are male and ride the bus. What is the joint relative frequency of male bus riders

Joint relative frequency = joint frequency / grand total = 15/50 = 0.30 or 30%.

The marginal relative frequency for 'Female' in a two-way table is 0.55. What does this mean

A marginal relative frequency of 0.55 for 'Female' means that 55% of all the people surveyed are female.

A conditional relative frequency is calculated by dividing a joint frequency by:

Conditional relative frequency is found by dividing a joint frequency by the marginal frequency (total) of the row or column that represents the given condition.

In a two-way table, 60 out of 100 males prefer action movies. What is the conditional relative frequency of preferring action movies given that the person is male

Conditional relative frequency = joint frequency / marginal frequency of the condition = 60/100 = 0.60 or 60% of males prefer action movies.

Which relative frequency is found by dividing each cell by the grand total

Joint relative frequencies are calculated by dividing each cell's frequency by the grand total of all observations, giving the proportion each cell represents of the entire data set.

In a relative frequency table, the marginal relative frequencies for all rows should sum to:

The marginal relative frequencies for all rows sum to 1 (100%), because together the row categories account for all observations in the data set.

A two-way table has a joint relative frequency of 0.12 for 'Male and Plays Soccer.' The marginal relative frequency for 'Male' is 0.48. What is the conditional relative frequency of playing soccer given male

Conditional relative frequency = joint relative frequency / marginal relative frequency = 0.12 / 0.48 = 0.25 or 25% of males play soccer.

What is relative frequency

Relative frequency is the ratio of a particular frequency to the total number of observations. It expresses what proportion or percentage of the total a particular category represents.

What is a joint relative frequency

A joint relative frequency is found by dividing the frequency in a specific cell (the intersection of two categories) by the grand total of all observations in the table.

What is a marginal relative frequency

A marginal relative frequency is the ratio of a row total or column total to the grand total, representing the proportion of observations that fall into that single category regardless of the other variable.

What is a conditional relative frequency

A conditional relative frequency is found by dividing a joint frequency by a marginal frequency (row or column total), showing the proportion of one category within a specific subgroup of the other variable.

What is the difference between a frequency table and a relative frequency table

A frequency table displays raw counts of observations, while a relative frequency table converts those counts to proportions or percentages by dividing each frequency by the total number of observations.

What does a two-way relative frequency table show

A two-way relative frequency table displays proportions or percentages rather than raw counts, making it easier to compare categories of different sizes and identify patterns in the data.

What does a relative frequency of 0.01 represent

A relative frequency of 0.01 means that the category represents 1% of the total observations (0.01 = 1/100 = 1%).

A restaurant surveys 500 customers about dining preference (Dine-In, Takeout) and day of week (Weekday, Weekend). Weekday Dine-In=120, Weekday Takeout=180, Weekend Dine-In=130, Weekend Takeout=70. What is the joint relative frequency of Weekend Dine-In customers

Joint relative frequency = 130/500 = 0.26 or 26% of all customers are weekend dine-in customers.

A high school tracks 800 students by grade (Underclassman, Upperclassman) and extracurricular participation (Active, Inactive). Underclassman Active=180, Underclassman Inactive=220, Upperclassman Active=280, Upperclassman Inactive=120. What is the conditional relative frequency of being active given that the student is an upperclassman

Upperclassman total: 280 + 120 = 400. Conditional relative frequency of active given upperclassman = 280/400 = 0.70 or 70%.

A fitness center surveys 300 members about class preference (Yoga, Spin, Weights) and time slot (Morning, Evening). Morning: Yoga=50, Spin=30, Weights=40. Evening: Yoga=40, Spin=60, Weights=80. What is the marginal relative frequency of evening members

Evening total: 40 + 60 + 80 = 180. Marginal relative frequency = 180/300 = 0.60 or 60% of members prefer evening classes.

A hospital surveys 500 patients about visit type (Emergency, Scheduled) and insurance status (Insured, Uninsured). Emergency Insured=80, Emergency Uninsured=120, Scheduled Insured=250, Scheduled Uninsured=50. What is the conditional relative frequency of being uninsured given that the visit was an emergency

Emergency total: 80 + 120 = 200. Conditional relative frequency of uninsured given emergency = 120/200 = 0.60 or 60%.

A tech company surveys 600 employees about work arrangement (Remote, Hybrid, In-Office) and job satisfaction (High, Low). Remote: High=150, Low=50. Hybrid: High=120, Low=80. In-Office: High=100, Low=100. Create the joint relative frequency table and identify the largest joint relative frequency.

Remote High: 150/600 = 0.25. Hybrid High: 120/600 = 0.20. In-Office High: 100/600 = 0.167. Remote Low: 50/600 = 0.083. Hybrid Low: 80/600 = 0.133. In-Office Low: 100/600 = 0.167. The largest joint relative frequency is Remote & High at 0.25.
